stations.label: THE DAY I BECAME 500
stations.line: They expanded me from ninety names to five hundred and handed me the whole American economy to count — I have been counting ever since.
stations.era_photo: Black-and-white silver gelatin print, a woman in a tailored 1957 gray flannel suit standing on a wood-paneled trading floor lined with chalkboards and ticker-tape machines, natural window light, slight film grain, formal posture.

stations.label: THE FIRST APOCALYPSE
stations.line: Stagflation and oil shock cut me nearly in half, and I learned the lesson that has never once failed me — extremes correct.
stations.era_photo: Grainy 1974 Kodachrome color photograph, a woman in a wide-lapel earth-toned pantsuit seated at a cluttered desk under fluorescent light, muted brown-and-orange tones, paper stock quotes scattered, faded color cast typical of mid-70s film.

stations.label: BLACK MONDAY
stations.line: I fell twenty percent in a single session and my voice did not crack; by the next year the record was already broken again.
stations.era_photo: 1987 color film photograph with slight motion blur, a woman in a sharp-shouldered navy power suit with a silk blouse standing amid frantic traders in a New York exchange, harsh overhead lighting, warm 80s film saturation.

stations.label: THE DOT-COM PEAK
stations.line: They told me the old rules were dead and priced my technology names for eternity — I expelled the pretenders and kept counting.
stations.era_photo: Early digital photograph circa 2000, low resolution and slightly soft, a woman in a minimalist charcoal skirt suit standing before a wall of glowing CRT monitors displaying green tickers, cool office lighting.

stations.label: THE YEAR THEY BURIED ME
stations.line: Lehman was inside me when it died, and I carried the loss the way I carry everything — as the weighted average, not the wound.
stations.era_photo: High-resolution digital photograph, a woman in an impeccable black tailored suit standing composed before a red-drenched market display board, deep shadows, sharp DSLR clarity, cold blue-and-crimson lighting.

stations.label: THE FASTEST CRASH
stations.line: A virus took a third of me in thirty-three days and then I made new highs before the year was out — calm the way tectonic plates are calm.
stations.era_photo: Digital photograph, a woman in a sleek dark suit alone in an empty glass-walled office, a face mask resting on the desk, laptop and multiple screens glowing, stark daylight through floor-to-ceiling windows, crisp 2020 clarity.

stations.label: THE MONOCULTURE QUESTION
stations.line: Technology now owns more than forty percent of my weight, and in private I wonder whether I am still an index or simply a tech fund in a blue-chip disguise.
stations.era_photo: Ultra-sharp modern digital photograph, a woman in a precise slate-gray designer suit standing before a vast curved LED display dominated by a few enormous tech logos, high dynamic range, clean cool studio lighting.

stations.label: STILL THE SCOREBOARD
stations.line: I have survived every crisis that was supposed to end me, and today, as always, my close is the only truth that needs no footnote.
stations.era_photo: Contemporary high-end digital portrait, a woman in a timeless tailored dark suit standing at the center of a modern trading floor bathed in soft closing-bell golden light, shallow depth of field, magazine-quality sharpness.
